  • Payable July 2; for shareholders of record June 5; ex-div June 5.
  • See TMTNF Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Woodside Energy Group reports Q1 results
data.description: • Woodside Energy Group press release (WDS): Q1 revenue of $3.26B (-1.8% Y/Y) beats by $200M.

data.title: Rush Street Interactive forecasts $1.49B-$1.54B 2026 revenue while advancing Alberta July 13 launch
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rush Street Interactive (RSI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “For the first quarter, we generated record revenue of $370 million, up 41% year-over-year, and a record adjusted EBITDA of $60 million, up 81% year-over-year.” (Co-Founder, CEO & Director Richard Schwartz)
  • “In our North American online casino markets, specifically, MAU growth reached 62%, eclipsing the 51% growth we just experienced last quarter.” (CEO Schwartz)
  • “This quarter, we estimate we grew market share sequentially by around 90 basis points in the North American online casino markets where we operate.” (CEO Schwartz)
  • “The regulator has set July 13 as the launch date.” (CEO Schwartz)
  • “Record first quarter revenue of $370.4 million represents 41% year-over-year growth.” (President & CFO Kyle Sauers)
  • “We now expect revenue to be in the range of $1.49 billion to $1.54 billion.” (President & CFO Sauers)

Outlook

  • “We are raising our full year 2026 guidance.” (President & CFO Sauers) “We now expect revenue to be in the range of $1.49 billion to $1.54 billion, representing year-over-year growth of 31% to 36%.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • “We now expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $230 million to $250 million, representing year-over-year growth of 50% to 63%.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • “Moving forward, we have assumed that we will have a new temporary 16% tax from mid-March through the end of the year when considering our raised guidance.” (CEO Schwartz)
  • Compared with the prior call’s baseline assumptions, management changed Alberta from “not included in guidance” to being included in 2026 guidance and changed Colombia from assuming a full-year 19% tax on revenue to assuming a temporary 16% tax from mid-March through year-end. (Prior call: President & CFO Sauers said, “we assume that this new 19% tax on revenue will be in place for the full year 2026,” and also said Alberta was “not included in guidance.”)

Financial Results

  • “Adjusted EBITDA reached a record $60.2 million, representing 81% year-over-year growth and over 16% margins.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • “Gross margins came in at 35.7%.” (President & CFO Sauers) “Marketing expenses in the quarter were $46.2 million.” (President & CFO Sauers) “G&A for the first quarter was $25.8 million.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • “North America ARPMAU was $317 in the first quarter, down 14% year-over-year.” (President & CFO Sauers) “In Latin America, our ARPMAU for the first quarter was $54, up 51% year-over-year.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • “In the first quarter, online casino revenue grew 39% and online sports betting revenue grew 47%.” (President & CFO Sauers) “Regionally, revenue in North America grew 26% in the first quarter and revenue in Latin America grew 134%.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • “Our balance sheet remains strong with $331 million in cash on hand as of March 31, and we still have 0 debt on our books.” (President & CFO Sauers)

Q&A

  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan Chase & Co: “Can you just talk about who these customers are that you're acquiring?” (President & CFO Sauers) “I'd say the players look a lot like the ones that we've acquired before.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan Chase & Co: “Can you talk about… what you're underwriting… in terms of market share?” (President & CFO Sauers) “I don't think we want to put a market share bogey out there just yet.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • Bernard McTernan, Needham: “What customer acquisitions channels are working in particular?” (President & CFO Sauers) “The ones that are working exceptionally well, I'm not sure that I want to highlight that on a public call, though.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • Bernard McTernan, Needham: “Any commentary on what's contemplated in the guide” for the World Cup? (President & CFO Sauers) “We have built in some upside from the World Cup.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • Jordan Bender, Citizens JMP: “What the exit rate is… exiting the VAT tax?” (President & CFO Sauers) “We're pretty confident that… we are taking share.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • Ryan Sigdahl, Craig-Hallum: “How… is that acceleration coming… despite disciplined spend?” (President & CFO Sauers) “I wouldn't say it's getting easier.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • Michael Hickey, The Benchmark Company: “Competitive dynamics… in Mexico… take meaningful share?” (CEO Schwartz) “We really like Mexico for the opportunity to really differentiate on the casino side.” (CEO Schwartz)
  • Zachary Silverberg, Wells Fargo: “Competitors had their gaming license blocked… opportunity to kind of take share?” (CEO Schwartz) “Their exit from the markets certainly have helped us to acquire some customers from them.” (CEO Schwartz)
  • Chad Beynon, Macquarie: “Virginia… higher likelihood in '27?” (CEO Schwartz) “It definitely would be a very exciting market for us, and we are very interested in that opportunity.” (CEO Schwartz)
  • David Katz, Jefferies: “Where do you think an aspirational flow-through level could be?” (President & CFO Sauers) “We still think that we can get to kind of low to mid-20%.” (President & CFO Sauers)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with repeated probing on MAU sustainability, Alberta launch economics, Colombia tax/election risk, and Mexico competitive dynamics; the tone included pointed asks such as “how… is that acceleration coming” (Ryan Sigdahl, Craig-Hallum) and “blocked… opportunity to kind of take share” (Zachary Silverberg, Wells Fargo).
  • Management: positive and confident in prepared remarks, anchored by “record revenue” and “raising our full year 2026 guidance” (President & CFO Sauers), while selectively withholding details in Q&A, including “I'm not sure that I want to highlight that on a public call” (President & CFO Sauers).
  • Versus last quarter: management messaging kept a similar confidence level around “casino-first strategy,” but the current call shifted from conditional Alberta timing to a specific date (“July 13”) (CEO Schwartz) and from assuming a full-year Colombia 19% revenue tax to assuming a temporary 16% tax (CEO Schwartz).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance: the company moved from “revenue… $1.375 billion to $1.425 billion” and “adjusted EBITDA… $210 million to $230 million” (President & CFO Sauers, prior call) to “revenue… $1.49 billion to $1.54 billion” and “adjusted EBITDA… $230 million to $250 million” (President & CFO Sauers, current call).
  • Canada/Alberta: prior call framed Alberta timing as “end of Q2, early Q3” and “we don't have it in guidance” (CEO Schwartz; President & CFO Sauers, prior call), while the current call stated, “The regulator has set July 13 as the launch date,” and “our increased revenue and EBITDA guidance now includes the impact of the Alberta launch for the back half of the year.” (CEO Schwartz)
  • Colombia: prior call said, “we assume that this new 19% tax on revenue will be in place for the full year 2026” (President & CFO Sauers, prior call); current call emphasized, “the Constitutional Court determined the 19% VAT to be unconstitutional” and updated assumptions to a “temporary 16% tax… through the end of the year.” (CEO Schwartz)

Risks and Concerns

  • “This new emergency decree and associated tax decree will also undergo a new and distinct review by the Constitutional Court during the coming months.” (CEO Schwartz)
  • “Certainly, we're watching it very closely.” (President & CFO Sauers) “There's an election coming up… the final election will be in June.” (President & CFO Sauers)
  • “We expect to begin investing in marketing and brand building ahead of our launch in Alberta… in the second quarter.” (CEO Schwartz)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a step-change quarter driven by accelerating North American iCasino user growth and improved marketing efficiency, alongside outsized Latin America performance, and paired that with higher 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance that now incorporates Alberta’s July 13 launch and assumes a temporary 16% Colombia tax from mid-March through year-end while the regulatory and political review process remains in motion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wednesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
7:00 AM MBA Mortgage Applications

The Mortgage Bankers' Association compiles various mortgage loan indexes. The purchase applications index measures applications at mortgage lenders.

8:30 AM Durable Goods Orders

Durable goods orders are new orders placed with domestic manufacturers for factory hard goods.

A moderate increase seen with orders expected up 0.5 percent and 0.4 percent, ex-transportation, in March from February.

8:30 AM Housing Starts and Permits

Housing starts measure the initial construction of single-family and multi-family units on a monthly basis.

Rising cost pressures expected to depress housing starts to an annual 1.37 million in February and 1.40 million rate in March from 1.487 million in January. 

8:30 AM International Trade in Goods (Advance)

This monthly report offers advance import and export data on the goods components of the monthly trade report.

Goods trade gap seen up at $87.8 billion in March versus $84.6 billion in February.

8:30 AM Retail Inventories (Advance)

Retail inventories measure the monthly dollar value of inventories held by retailers.

8:30 AM Wholesale Inventories (Advance)

Wholesale inventories measure the monthly dollar value of inventories held by merchant wholesalers and are tracked to gauge inventory change in quarterly GDP.

10:30 AM EIA Petroleum Status Report

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on petroleum inventories in the U.S., whether produced here or abroad.

11:00 AM Survey of Business Uncertainty

The survey of business uncertainty is a panel survey measuring year-ahead expectations that US firms have about their own sales and employment growth. 

2:00 PM FOMC Announcement

The FOMC meeting announcement is a policy statement issued at the conclusion of each meeting of the Federal Open Market Committee. 

Fed seen still in wait and see mode and no doubt pleased that the job market appears to have stabilized. Still watching for progress on inflation after the tariff episode, now interrupted by an energy price shock.

2:30 PM Fed Chair Press Conference 

The Fed announced in 2011 that then Fed Chair Ben Bernanke would hold press briefings four times a year to explain the FOMC's latest quarterly economic projections.

data.title: Rocky Brands forecasts ~6% 2026 revenue growth as tariffs ease and gross margins move back above 40%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rocky Brands (RCKY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Q1 sales increased 9%, following the 9% increase we achieved in the fourth quarter of 2025," said (Chairman, CEO & President Jason Brooks), adding that growth was driven by "legacy styles and compelling new product introductions" and that the company is seeing "consistent full price selling" across key accounts, digital partners, and its own sites.

  • (CEO Brooks) tied near-term profitability pressure to trade policy and framed the cadence of improvement: "The year-over-year change in gross and operating margins was driven primarily by higher tariffs" and "the headwind from higher tariff starts to lessen in the second quarter," which he said supports "returning gross margins to 40% range" and "delivering meaningful earnings growth in the second half of the year."

  • On brand-level momentum, (CEO Brooks) said "XTRATUF started 2026 with exceptional momentum," "Muck delivered its best first quarter in over 3 years," and Durango posted "single-digit growth." He described Georgia Boot as "a slight single-digit decline" that was "primarily timing driven" and said Rocky Work, Outdoor & Western was positive as wholesale strengthened "through greater in-line product sales versus last year's off-price focus."

  • (COO, CFO & Treasurer Thomas Robertson) anchored the quarter’s headline figures: "Reported net sales for the first quarter increased 9.1% year-over-year to $124.4 million," and he emphasized the key driver of margin compression: "The 470 basis point decrease was driven by a little over $7 million in higher tariffs."

Outlook

  • (CFO Robertson) said, "based on our first quarter performance, we are reiterating our full year 2026 guidance provided on our fourth quarter call" and guided that the company "continue[s] to expect revenue to increase approximately 6% over 2025" and that "this translates into EPS growth in the low-teen range."

  • (CFO Robertson) updated the tariff timing assumptions versus the prior quarter’s framing, stating the roughly $10 million tariff impact in the first half is now "split roughly 70-30 between Q1 and Q2 versus our prior view of 80-20," and added Q2 margin improvement is still expected but "will not be as meaningful as we anticipated at the start of the year."

  • For quarterly cadence, (CFO Robertson) said, "we now expect Q2 EPS to be down somewhere in the neighborhood of $0.20 versus Q2 last year" and reiterated the back-half setup: "We look forward to having the current tariff headwinds largely behind us as we exit Q2, which will drive gross margins back above 40%."

Financial Results

  • (CFO Robertson) reported segment detail within the $124.4 million quarter: "wholesale sales increased $3.6 million or 4.8% to $78.4 million" and "retail sales increased 16.5% and to $42.7 million," with "contract manufacturing sales" of "$3.3 million."

  • On profitability, (CFO Robertson) said gross profit was "$45.4 million or 36.5% of sales" and attributed the year-over-year margin decline primarily to tariffs, while noting offsets including "strong full-price selling," "favorable channel mix with higher retail sales," and "price increases implemented in Q2 of 2025."

  • (CFO Robertson) said operating costs rose with mix: "The increase in operating expenses was driven primarily by higher logistics costs associated with the increase in retail sales." He reported "income from operations was $3.6 million" and that on a GAAP basis the company delivered "net income of $1.3 million or $0.17 per diluted share."

  • On balance sheet and inventory positioning, (CFO Robertson) said cash was "$1.7 million" and debt totaled "$122.2 million." He added, "Inventories at the end of the first quarter were $172.6 million" and said management was "pleased with our inventory management" while "maintaining appropriate stock levels to support our growth."

Q&A

  • Jonathan Komp, Baird: asked about demand amid uncertainty; (Chairman, CEO & President Jason Brooks) responded, "we feel pretty positive" across several brands, while flagging that "the one area that we still aren't seeing a huge uptick is in commercial military, where we haven't seen any contracts from our U.S. government." (COO, CFO & Treasurer Robertson) added, "our order book looks very strong for the rest of the year" and said the company has "not seen a big change in behavior" from consumers.

  • Jonathan Komp, Baird: asked about freight and product cost pressures; (CFO Robertson) said, "we definitely experienced higher freight fuel surcharges" and highlighted input exposure: "there's a lot of oil-based products" in materials, adding, "We've seen some slight price increases" and "We're being warned of larger ones."

  • Jonathan Komp, Baird: asked what supports back-half earnings growth; (CFO Robertson) said, "the thing that's given us the most confidence is the order book" and described being "really up in future orders across all brands," particularly "our rubber products" (Muck and XTRATUF).

  • Jonathan Komp, Baird: asked about tariffs and refunds; (CFO Robertson) said, "the guidance that we provided assumes no refunds are captured" and called refunds "all upside," adding, "The total requests that we're seeking is about $20.5 million" and that the company is also watching "Section 301 investigations later this summer."

  • Janine Hoffman Stichter, BTIG: asked about sell-in vs. sell-through; (CEO Brooks) tied prebooks to retail performance, saying Q4 sell-through "allowed them" to be "more comfortable in their bookings for Q3 and Q4." (CFO Robertson) said, "arguably the best booking season we've ever had" for new product.

  • Bruce Geller, Geller Ventures: pressed on tariff-adjusted earnings power; (CFO Robertson) replied, "I think that's a fair statement" to the characterization of Q1 EPS impact and said, "your logic is correct" while noting uncertainty with "10% tariffs" and potential Section 301 changes.

  • Bruce Geller, Geller Ventures: asked about XTRATUF expansion in Western retail; (CEO Brooks) said there has been "a little bit of expansion" and that management sees "a really positive potential opportunity" in that category.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly skeptical and detail-seeking, focusing on demand durability, cost creep, and tariff math, including questions on refunds and earnings power such as, "Is that a fair statement?"

  • Management tone was slightly positive in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing momentum ("we feel pretty positive") while acknowledging constraints in specific areas like government contracts and near-term cost volatility.

  • Versus the prior quarter, management maintained optimism but gave more explicit caution that Q2 improvement "will not be as meaningful as we anticipated," while analysts broadened questioning from retail strength (Q4 focus) to tariff refunds, fuel surcharges, and materials inflation.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • In Q1, (CFO Robertson) reiterated full-year guidance from Q4 but adjusted tariff timing, shifting the first-half split to "70-30 between Q1 and Q2" from the prior "80-20" framing.

  • Q4 messaging emphasized strong retail acceleration and margin resilience; Q1 commentary emphasized that "higher tariffs" drove the year-over-year margin compression and that earnings growth is expected to be weighted to the second half after tariffs are "largely behind us as we exit Q2."

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s questions on retail upside and brand growth investments to Q1’s deeper scrutiny of cost inputs (fuel, oil-based materials), tariff refund mechanics, and visibility into the order book.

Risks and Concerns

  • (CEO Brooks) cited a softness point in government-driven demand: "we haven't seen any contracts from our U.S. government" in commercial military.

  • (CFO Robertson) highlighted cost risks from freight and inputs, saying fuel surcharges increased and that oil-based material costs have shown "slight price increases" with warnings of "larger ones."

  • (CFO Robertson) framed uncertainty around tariffs and refunds, noting the refund system "is not working perfectly" and that Section 301 developments could affect the tariff outlook.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a continuation of late-2025 momentum, led by broad-based strength across key brands and D2C/retail growth, while describing profitability as temporarily constrained by tariffs and logistics costs. The company reiterated its full-year revenue growth outlook of approximately 6% and low-teen EPS growth, updated the timing of first-half tariff pressure into Q2, and pointed to a strong order book and potential tariff refunds—"about $20.5 million" requested—as key swing factors as it targets gross margins moving back above 40% after Q2.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: South Plains signals Bank of Houston deal to be 11% accretive in 2027 with tangible book value earn-back under 3 years
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: South Plains Financial (SPFI) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We delivered solid first quarter results, highlighted by strong profitability, continued improvement in credit quality and disciplined balance sheet management" (Chairman & CEO Curtis Griffith).

  • "Our strategy remains focused on expanding our lending team across our high-growth Texas markets while also pursuing accretive M&A" (Chairman & CEO Curtis Griffith). Griffith added, "we have not identified another transaction that meets our strict criteria" and "we're not interested in growth for growth's sake."

  • On the Bank of Houston (BOH) acquisition, Griffith said, "We were pleased to complete our merger on April 1," and "we expect the core conversion to be completed in early May." He also reiterated, "we continue to expect the merger to be 11% accretive to our earnings in 2027 with a tangible book value earn-back of less than 3 years."

  • "Loans held for investment decreased by $41 million to $3.1 billion" and "we experienced strong unfunded loan commitment growth during the quarter" (President & Director Cory Newsom). Newsom also said, "we remain confident in delivering our loan growth guidance for the full year, albeit towards the lower end of our mid- to high single-digit range."

  • "For the first quarter, diluted earnings per share were $0.85 compared to $0.90 from the linked quarter" (CFO & Treasurer Steven Crockett).

Outlook

  • "Importantly, our loan pipeline remains healthy" and "we also expect another early payoff of approximately $34 million multifamily loan" (President & Director Cory Newsom).

  • "We do expect our loan yields to moderate in the quarters ahead" (President Newsom).

  • "We would expect deposit growth to be flat to down in the second quarter before returning to growth in the second half of 2026 before you factor in acquisition deposits" (CFO Crockett).

  • Compared with last quarter’s stance that "you could see a little bit of compression" in NIM (CFO Steven Crockett, Q4 2025), management’s current-quarter positioning emphasized stability, with Crockett saying, "our goal is to maintain our profitability at current levels while growing our balance sheet."

Financial results

  • "Net interest income was $43 million for the first quarter" and "our net interest margin on a tax equivalent basis was 4.04%" (CFO Steven Crockett).

  • "Deposits increased by $154 million or 4% from the linked quarter to $4.03 billion" and "our cost of deposits decreased by 4 basis points to 1.97%" (CFO Crockett).

  • Credit provisioning and asset-quality commentary centered on lower reserve build: "We recorded a $260,000 provision for credit losses" and "the decrease in provision expense was largely attributable to the decrease in loan balances, combined with a decrease of $4.8 million in nonperforming loans and a $460,000 decrease in loan net charge-offs" (CFO Crockett).

  • Noninterest income and fee items included mortgage strength and an investment loss: "We generated $11.3 million of noninterest income" and "the increase... was primarily due to an increase of $1.5 million in mortgage banking revenues, partially offset by a loss of approximately $800,000 in an SBIC investment" (President Newsom).

  • Expenses reflected acquisition and compensation pressure: "Our noninterest expense increased $2.5 million to $35.5 million" and "there was approximately $1.5 million in acquisition-related expenses" (CFO Crockett).

Q&A

  • Wood Lay, KBW: asked about BOH balance-sheet repositioning and why it didn’t change the accretion outlook; CFO Crockett responded, "some of the Federal Home Loan Bank borrowings had dropped" and "some of the brokered -- time broker deposits did not get redone," adding, "we'll continue to look at the noncore funding where we can and pare that back."

  • Wood Lay, KBW: pressed on how quickly BOH deposit costs could be lowered and whether it could support NIM; Chairman & CEO Griffith answered, "as all of the higher cost stuff hits maturity and payoff dates... we'll just pay them off," while also cautioning, "it's not going to be a radical improvement in overall deposit costs for us."

  • Brett Rabatin, StoneX: asked if new lender hires were entering specialized businesses; President Newsom replied, "there's not a single one that we've hired that's going to put us into something that we don't think we have good expertise in doing or gets us out of the fairway that we like to stay in."

  • Brett Rabatin, StoneX: asked about mortgage outlook; Senior VP & Chief Credit Officer Brent Bates said, "I think rates probably have to drop quite a bit to make a meaningful difference there," and Newsom added, "we've kept the nucleus of this business together, and we're not losing any money."

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: asked whether payoffs were anticipated in guidance and about BOH synergy timing; Chief Credit Officer Bates said, "we anticipated this... it was kind of baked in," and Newsom said on integration, "we closed, we have converted and integrated everything about this inside of a quarter... we're going to do a conversion May 8."

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: asked about energy exposure; Chief Credit Officer Bates answered, "most of our energy portfolio is really on the C&I servicing side" and "we're still under 5% of the portfolio."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-skeptical, repeatedly pressing on margin durability, BOH funding repricing, and loan-growth math, including Yanchunis asking, "I'm just trying to understand what the jumping off point is" and Scouten testing the implied pace of growth.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive but measured, frequently emphasizing patience and discipline, including Newsom saying, "this is a bit more of a marathon than a sprint" and Griffith signaling caution on macro uncertainty while maintaining strategic confidence.

  • Versus Q4 2025’s tone, which highlighted broad full-year performance and anticipated acceleration, Q1 2026 included more operational integration detail and more explicit near-term caveats (payoffs, tax-related deposit seasonality), while still reiterating the same BOH accretion and payback framework.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The BOH narrative shifted from planning to execution: Q4 2025 said the merger was "expected to occur early in the second quarter of 2026" (Chairman & CEO Curtis Griffith, Q4 2025), while Q1 2026 reported, "We were pleased to complete our merger on April 1" and set "core conversion" for "early May" (Chairman & CEO Griffith).

  • Loan-growth framing tightened: Q4 2025 said loan growth was expected to "accelerate to a mid- to high single-digit growth rate in 2026" (Chairman & CEO Griffith, Q4 2025), while Q1 2026 guided to delivery "towards the lower end" of that range (President Newsom).

  • NIM language moved from acknowledging potential pressure ("you could see a little bit of compression," CFO Steven Crockett, Q4 2025) to emphasizing steadier profitability goals ("maintain our profitability at current levels," CFO Crockett).

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited macro uncertainty: "inflationary pressures appear to be resurfacing" and this "may limit the Federal Reserve's ability to further reduce interest rates" and "could act as a headwind to economic activity and loan growth" (Chairman & CEO Griffith).

  • Loan growth was described as facing payoff headwinds: "some large payoffs will continue to be a headwind to loan growth" (President Newsom).

  • Deposit flows were expected to be seasonally pressured: "we expect a portion of the public funds to flow back out of the Bank" in Q2 due to "annual tax payments" (CFO Crockett).

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a transition quarter that combined stable core earnings drivers with BOH integration execution: Griffith said the merger was completed April 1, the core conversion was expected in early May, and the company continued to expect 11% earnings accretion in 2027 with a tangible book value earn-back under 3 years. While Newsom reiterated full-year loan growth expectations, he positioned delivery toward the lower end of the mid- to high single-digit range due to multifamily payoffs, as Crockett described near-term deposit seasonality and a priority on maintaining profitability as the balance sheet grows.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Opko outlines Q2 2026 revenue of $127M-$132M as ModeX advances 5 clinical programs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OPKO Health (OPK) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Dr. Phillip Frost (Chairman & CEO) said OPKO’s “particular emphasis” in Q1 was “advancing our ModeX product development pipeline,” adding that “ModeX now has 5 programs in the clinic” and that OPKO “dosed our first subjects in the Phase I clinical trial of MDX2301” and “announced the dosing of the first patient” for MDX2003.

  • Dr. Phillip Frost (Chairman & CEO) described partnerships as a funding lever, saying the Regeneron collaboration “is a good source of nondilutive capital,” and stating, “The potential total value of the Regeneron collaboration exceeds $1 billion in milestones plus future royalties.”

  • Dr. Elias Zerhouni (President & Vice Chairman) highlighted upcoming catalysts and timelines, saying Merck “expect[s] … the data needed to inform a Phase II design by the end of this year” with “initiation of a Phase II clinical study anticipated next year,” while OPKO expects to present “Phase Ia data” for MDX2001 “in the second half of this year.”

  • Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) emphasized liquidity and capital return, saying, “We ended the quarter with … over $341 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash,” and adding, “as of the end of Q1, we had approximately $108 million authorized to repurchase shares of our common stock.”

Outlook

  • Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) guided Q2 2026 total revenue to “between $127 million to $132 million,” with “revenue of services from $72 million to $76 million,” “Pharmaceutical product revenue of $38 million to $42 million,” and “IP and other revenue … between $15 million to $19 million, including the Pfizer profit share of $6 million to $8 million.”

  • Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) guided Q2 total costs and expenses to “between $180 million and $190 million,” and said OPKO expects “R&D to become between $32 million and $38 million, partially offset by $5 million to $7 million in BARDA funding,” with “depreciation and amortization expense of approximately $24 million.”

  • On continuity vs. last quarter, Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) said, “We’re affirming our full year guidance that we introduced when we reported our Q4 results.”

Financial results

  • OPKO reported Q1 2026 consolidated results of “total revenues … $124.2 million,” “consolidated operating loss … $51 million,” and “net loss … $54.8 million or $0.07 per share,” according to Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer).

  • In Diagnostics, Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) reported “Revenue for Q1 2026 was $72.2 million, including $6.5 million from our 4Kscore test,” and said “operating loss was $13 million,” while adding the company remains “on track to achieve breakeven … by the middle of the year.”

  • In Pharmaceuticals, Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) reported segment revenue of “$52 million,” including “product sales … $38 million,” “Pfizer profit share … $6.4 million,” and “BARDA funding … $4 million,” and said segment R&D was “$28.8 million” with a segment “operating loss … $30 million.”

Q&A

  • Maurice Raycroft, Jefferies: asked what proportion of payer policies reflect the new 4Kscore label and how OPKO will drive primary care adoption; Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) said OPKO is “starting with Medicare to update their coverage determination,” and added, “we are waiting for that LCD to get updated before we more aggressively go into the primary care market,” expecting it “in the middle of this year.”

  • Maurice Raycroft, Jefferies: asked about growth expectations and 4Kscore contribution; Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) said OPKO expects “double-digit growth on 4K volumes this year,” tied to BioReference reaching “that range of $300 million to $312 million for the total business of BioReference.”

  • Maurice Raycroft, Jefferies: asked about NGENLA/GENOTROPIN trends and switching; Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) said conversions are “not as aggressively as we previously had expected, but certainly ramping upwards,” and added Pfizer “doesn’t necessarily give us anything forward-looking.”

  • Lut Ming Cheng, JPMorgan: asked what OPKO seeks in early MDX2003 data; Gary Nabel (Chief Information Officer & Director) said, “the first order of business is to show acceptable toxicity,” then focus on “failures to the CD20 multispecifics or bispecifics,” and later evaluate “opportunity to be used in autoimmunity.”

  • Lut Ming Cheng, JPMorgan: asked about EBV vaccine next steps and disclosures; Gary Nabel (Chief Information Officer & Director) said, “information will be available certainly by the end of the year,” while emphasizing, “these are really decisions that Merck will make.”

  • Yi Chen, H.C. Wainwright: asked why Q1 revenue came in below prior guidance; Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) said the shortfall was “related to our other revenue and IP revenue,” with “almost $6 million” tied to delayed BARDA CMC activities, and added, “from a net bottom line perspective, no impact.”

  • Yi Chen, H.C. Wainwright: asked about MDX2301 commercialization; Dr. Elias Zerhouni (President & Vice Chairman) said OPKO is “looking for a partner,” and Gary Nabel (Chief Information Officer & Director) added, “BARDA would want to stockpile,” and it is “too early to say what commercial partner.”

  • Kevin DeGeeter, Ladenburg Thalmann: asked differentiation and milestones for in vivo CAR-T; Dr. Elias Zerhouni (President & Vice Chairman) emphasized covalent antibody targeting of LNPs and said effects were seen at “5 to 10x lower doses than the competition,” adding CMC should be finished in “3, 4 months, 5 months,” with hopes for a human trial “before the end of this year.”

  • Edward Tenthoff, Piper Sandler: asked whether in vivo CAR-T Phase I would run in China and about partnerships; Dr. Elias Zerhouni (President & Vice Chairman) said OPKO has “a team … there interviewing the clinical sites and with a partner, local partner.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and detail-oriented, with repeated requests for timing and commercial clarity (e.g., “Any update in terms of data disclosure perhaps later this year?” and “how we should think about growth expectations”).

  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was positive and milestone-driven (e.g., “we expect ModeX to achieve a number of clinical and partnership milestones”), while in Q&A it was more conditional around external decision-makers and payer processes (e.g., “we are waiting for that LCD” and “these are really decisions that Merck will make”).

  • Versus Q4 2025, OPKO used more near-term execution language on clinical starts (“we dosed our first subjects”) rather than primarily forward scheduling (“to enter the clinic in the first half of 2026”), while maintaining similar caution on Merck-controlled disclosures.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The narrative shifted from Q4’s transformation framing (“first full quarter since closing” the Labcorp oncology sale) to Q1’s operational tuning, with Dr. Frost saying Diagnostics is “our second full quarter with the new BioReference footprint” and management emphasizing streamlining to reach mid-year breakeven.

  • ModeX progressed from “3 programs in the clinic already, and 2 more entering the clinic” (Q4) to “5 assets in the clinic” (Q1), alongside added specificity on planned readouts (MDX2001 Phase Ia data “in the second half of this year”).

  • Q&A focus remained consistent on 4Kscore reimbursement/primary care expansion and on Pfizer’s NGENLA trajectory, with Q1 adding deeper commercialization questions for MDX2301 and execution questions on China trial plans for in vivo CAR-T.

Risks and concerns

  • Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) said Q1 revenue was impacted by delays in BARDA CMC activity: “those got delayed until later this year.”

  • Reimbursement timing risk for expanding 4Kscore into primary care remained central, with Adam Logal (Senior VP, CFO, Chief Accounting Officer & Treasurer) saying OPKO is “waiting for that LCD to get updated.”

  • Clinical and partnership dependency risk was repeatedly underscored, with Gary Nabel (Chief Information Officer & Director) noting EBV vaccine advancement and disclosures are “decisions that Merck will make,” while MDX2003’s near-term focus is “acceptable toxicity.”

Final takeaway

OPKO management emphasized liquidity, buybacks, and an expanding ModeX clinical footprint, while positioning Diagnostics for mid-year breakeven driven by operational efficiencies and a reimbursement-dependent 4Kscore primary care expansion. For Q2, the company guided total revenue of $127 million to $132 million alongside higher R&D spending, and it highlighted multiple catalyst windows across oncology, infectious disease, and in vivo CAR-T—while repeatedly noting that payer timelines, BARDA activity timing, and Merck-controlled EBV decisions remain key swing factors.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Clearwater Paper outlines $1.4B-$1.5B revenue outlook while projecting Q2 adjusted EBITDA of breakeven to (-$10M)
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Clearwater Paper Corporation (CLW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our shipment volumes were up 5%, which was more than offset by lower market pricing, resulting in net sales being down 5% compared to the prior year,” said CEO Arsen Kitch, adding, “Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $2 million, slightly above our guidance of breakeven,” and noting “approximately $15 million in weather-related impacts at our mills earlier in the quarter.” (CEO, President & Director Arsen Kitch)
  • “We restructured our Cypress Bend, Arkansas facility, resulting in a reduction of approximately 20% of roles at the mill,” Kitch said, adding, “We're planning to run the mill at reduced operating run rates until industry conditions improve,” and, “This action will drive an expected cost reduction of approximately $8 million to $12 million on an annualized basis.” (CEO Kitch)
  • “This quarter, we launched Velora, a new lightweight folding carton paperboard brand that is engineered to compete with imported FBB,” Kitch said, and added the company is also evaluating portfolio moves: “We continue to evaluate our CUK investment decision,” and “we are evaluating opportunities to add CRB to our product portfolio.” (CEO Kitch)
  • “For the quarter, we reported a net loss from continuing operations of $13 million or $1.29 per diluted share,” and “Net sales were $360 million,” CFO Sherri Baker said, adding, “SG&A as a percentage of sales remained below our target range of 6% to 7%.” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Sherri Baker)

Outlook

  • “For the second quarter, we expect adjusted EBITDA in the range of breakeven to negative $10 million,” Baker said, citing “our planned major maintenance outage at our Lewiston facility, which will have a direct cost of $22 million to $24 million,” plus “$5 million to $7 million of higher input costs.” (CFO Baker)
  • “Our full year assumptions remain as follows: revenue of $1.4 billion to $1.5 billion,” Baker said, also guiding to “capital expenditures of $65 million to $75 million,” “targeted working capital improvement of $20 million to $30 million,” and “Major maintenance outage costs of $45 million to $50 million,” while noting, “the Cypress Bend outage has been moved from Q2 to Q4 of this year.” (CFO Baker)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s focus on being “prepared to take market-related downtime,” the current call added a quantified, conflict-driven cost item: “we're projecting $3 million to $5 million of quarterly headwinds from these cost increases until the conflict is resolved and global supply chains have returned to normal,” Kitch said. (CEO Kitch)

Financial Results

  • Baker said Q1 results included “$17.5 million of insurance proceeds,” and that the severe weather event “impacted EBITDA by approximately $15 million in the quarter.” (CFO Baker)
  • On acquisition-related insurance, Baker said, “In the first quarter, we received a second settlement payment of more than $17 million,” and, “As of March 31, approximately $50 million of the policy limit remains.” (CFO Baker)
  • Baker added a free-cash-flow framing for the year: “Importantly, we believe that we have a path to breakeven or better free cash flow for the year,” driven by “insurance recoveries, a tax refund that we are expecting and reductions in net working capital.” (CFO Baker)

Q&A

  • Sean Steuart, TD Cowen: Asked whether the Cypress Bend restructuring represented Clearwater’s full supply response and how fast broader capacity cuts could arrive; CEO Kitch replied, “We intend to run the mill at reduced operating rates until industry conditions improve,” and described a new operating posture: “we have about 1.2 million tons of annual production that we're comfortable with after taking this action.” (CEO Kitch)
  • Sean Steuart, TD Cowen: Asked about tax refund size and timing; CFO Baker answered, “the overall for the full year would be $27 million to $28 million, of which we received $4 million in the first quarter.” (CFO Baker)
  • Sean Steuart, TD Cowen: Asked whether Moody’s downgrade affects borrowing costs; CFO Baker said, “It would be more applicable to any future refinancings.” (CFO Baker)
  • Matthew McKellar, RBC Capital Markets: Pressed on the $3 million to $5 million per quarter cost headwind and recoveries; CEO Kitch said, “it is a sequential comparison,” and pointed to mitigation: “the Cypress Bend restructure should deliver about $2 million a quarter of cost reduction sequentially,” alongside “implementing a $60 price increase on our extruded products.” (CEO Kitch)
  • Matthew McKellar, RBC Capital Markets: Asked how Velora fits and potential volume; CEO Kitch said, “It is not incremental growth. It will take up some of our existing SBS capacity,” and, “We don't expect it to be a large number in the near term.” (CEO Kitch)
  • Michael Roxland, Truist Securities: Asked about customer response to the $60/ton extruded increase; CEO Kitch replied, “I'm not prepared to comment on feedback yet,” and added, “we are sold out.” (CEO Kitch)
  • Michael Roxland, Truist Securities: Asked what would trigger a CUK conversion and CRB optionality; CEO Kitch said, “It's a $60 million investment, when we're working very hard at this point in the cycle to remain cash flow neutral,” and for CRB, “it's a matter of either looking at M&A in the long run or looking at some additional partnerships or supply agreements.” (CEO Kitch)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative and pressing, focusing on durability of a recovery and financial flexibility, including “what would get you over the hump to pull the trigger” on CUK and what happens if “the biggest player refuses” to cut capacity. (Michael Roxland, Truist Securities)
  • Management tone was slightly positive but defensive on near-term pricing and demand visibility, including “I'm not prepared to comment on feedback yet,” while reiterating confidence with “I remain confident that this cycle will turn.” (CEO Kitch)
  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on “ample liquidity” and potential “extended curtailments,” the current call added more near-term caution around cash deployment, with Kitch saying, “$60 million right now is a bit of a stretch.” (CEO Kitch)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Strategy moved from planning for supply management (“may include extended curtailments”) to executing a specific asset action: “We restructured our Cypress Bend, Arkansas facility,” and “run the mill at reduced operating run rates.” (CEO Kitch)
  • Product roadmap progressed from preparation to execution on lightweight paperboard: in Q4 the company said it was “preparing to launch [Valora] ... in the second quarter,” while in Q1 Kitch said, “we launched Velora.” (CEO Kitch)
  • Guidance framing shifted from a Q1 adjusted EBITDA expectation of “approximately breakeven” to Q2 adjusted EBITDA “breakeven to negative $10 million,” explicitly tied to “$22 million to $24 million” outage cost. (CFO Baker)

Risks and Concerns

  • Kitch said the company is facing “industry capacity substantially exceeding demand by more than 10%,” and warned, “today's margin levels are resulting in negative operating cash flow after the CapEx that's required to maintain these assets.” (CEO Kitch)
  • Management flagged geopolitical cost inflation: “we're projecting $3 million to $5 million of quarterly headwinds,” and Baker added these dynamics “remain a near-term headwind to margins.” (CEO Kitch; CFO Baker)
  • Refinancing and maturity management remained in focus: “Our ABL goes current later this year,” Baker said, adding, “It is our intent to extend or refinance both instruments before they go current.” (CFO Baker)

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a Q1 shaped by pricing pressure and weather disruption, followed by a sharper focus on cash protection through a Cypress Bend restructuring, targeted pricing actions (including a revised $60 per ton extruded increase), and a full-year framework that kept revenue at $1.4 billion to $1.5 billion while aiming for “breakeven or better free cash flow for the year,” alongside plans to address upcoming debt refinancings.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Starbucks raises FY2026 guidance to 5%+ global comps and $2.25-$2.45 EPS as China deal closes
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Starbucks (SBUX) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • “Q2 marked a milestone for the business. We delivered growth on both the top and bottom line for the first time in more than 2 years.” (Chairman & CEO Brian Niccol)

  • “Positive comp trends have continued through April, and this gives us the confidence to take up our fiscal 2026 guidance for global comp growth to 5% or better and earnings per share to $2.25 to $2.45.” (Chairman & CEO Niccol)

  • “North America led our comp performance with both North America and U.S. comps accelerating to more than 7%, driven by over 4 percentage points of transaction growth.” (Chairman & CEO Niccol)

  • “In May, we're rolling out a new feature in our app that lets customers schedule their order pickup time.” (Chairman & CEO Niccol)

  • “During the quarter, we announced we would shift to weekly pay and introduced a new quarterly reward program for baristas and shift supervisors.” (Chairman & CEO Niccol)

  • “Our Q2 consolidated revenue was $9.5 billion, up 8% (sic) [ 9% ] to the prior year.” (Executive VP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Catherine Smith)

  • “Beginning in Q3, the retail operations of Starbucks China will be deconsolidated from our financials and will be reported as part of our broader licensed portfolio.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

Outlook

  • “We are raising our fiscal 2026 global comp guidance to 5% growth or better, led by 5% or better comps in the U.S.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

  • “We now expect our consolidated fiscal 2026 net revenues to be roughly flat year-over-year.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

  • “We continue to expect slight year-over-year growth in our fiscal 2026 consolidated operating margins.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

  • “We remain on track with our $2 billion cost savings plan.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

  • “We are raising our EPS guidance at both ends of the range to between $2.25 and $2.45.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

  • “We still expect to add approximately 600 to 650 net new coffeehouses this fiscal year.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

Financial results

  • “Q2 consolidated second quarter company revenue was $9.5 billion” and “Global comps were a strong 6%” alongside “consolidated operating margin improved to 9.4%.” (Chairman & CEO Brian Niccol)

  • “All in, Q2 earnings per share grew 22% year-over-year to $0.50, our first quarter of EPS growth in more than 2 years.” (Executive VP, CFO Catherine Smith)

  • “International operating margin grew by approximately 790 basis points to 20.3%” and “approximately half of our international margin expansion was driven by held-for-sale accounting related to Starbucks China, which temporarily reduced store operating expenses and D&A by approximately $118 million in the quarter.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

  • “In North America, our Q2 operating margin contracted approximately 170 basis points to 10.2%” and “North America margins in the quarter were also impacted by roughly 190 basis points of product and distribution cost increases as a percentage of revenues and greater-than-anticipated legal accruals.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

  • “Starbucks received approximately $3.1 billion in gross cash proceeds before taxes” and “Prior to closing, we repaid our $1 billion February maturities and expect to deploy the remaining proceeds toward additional debt reduction and ongoing balance sheet management.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

Q&A

  • Brian Harbour, Morgan Stanley: “I'm curious if you could talk about… the service times…”; Chairman & CEO Niccol: “4 minutes in the cafe, 4 minutes in the drive-thru and better than 12 minutes for promised times in mobile order pickup… right now, we've got about 80% of our stores or better hitting the metrics… we believe as we roll out the scheduled ordering, that's going to enable us to improve our performance.”

  • Sara Senatore, BofA Securities: “a 2-part question about the margin… impact of innovation on… cost of goods…”; Executive VP, CFO Smith: “we're also cognizant of the macro environment, and we want to make sure we're prudent… coffee elevation… almost… $1 a pound year-over-year change… and then the implications of tariffs… both of those we expect to abate toward the back end of the year.”

  • David Tarantino, Baird: “how much runway… operations improvements…”; Chairman & CEO Niccol: “We still have close to 40% of our stores where they're not at 4 shots… we only have done the uplift in about 300 stores… that's going to ramp up to well over 1,000 stores by the end of this year.”

  • David Palmer, Evercore ISI: “cost of the Green Apron Service… and… where this market overall is going”; Chairman & CEO Niccol: “when the category starts being talked about, the market leader benefits. And that's going to be us”; Executive VP, CFO Smith: “we'll annualize Green Apron Service in August… we're about the same level each quarter until then. And then obviously, it will come down quite a bit from there.”

  • Lauren Silberman, Deutsche Bank: “color on the cadence of comps… in April” and “what you would need to see to get towards the top end”; Chairman & CEO Niccol: “we really saw strength all quarter long… we haven't seen a lot of the macro effects trickle into consumer behavior as it relates to Starbucks”; Executive VP, CFO Smith: “it would need to lean on the plus… we need to see coffee prices abate… we'll continue to tighten up our innovation performance on COGS.”

  • Christopher O'Cull, Stifel: “Why do you think the company has been successful in engaging” lower-income cohorts; Chairman & CEO Niccol: “when you give them an experience that they feel is unique… we're seeing that play out with every income cohort… we have to demonstrate for people that it's worth it.”

  • Peter Saleh, BTIG: “ask about the rewards program… in April… disruption”; Chairman & CEO Niccol: “We fully expected there will be some disruption… But instead, it was just the opposite… we're already seen more rewards customers jump into the program.”

  • John Ivankoe, JPMorgan: “labor dollars per operating week… How much efficiency”; Chairman & CEO Niccol: “We don't see our way forward with cutting… we're going to be able to get 4 shots done in less than 30 seconds”; Executive VP, CFO Smith: “longer term, total hours, I wouldn't expect to necessarily go down… focusing on throughput… and then trying to be as effective and efficient as we can on the hours where they're not supporting a customer.”

  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: “EPS growth algorithm… $2 billion savings… flowing… to… $4 target”; Executive VP, CFO Smith: “we're really not changing our FY '28 targets at this time… It's really early.”

  • Sharon Zackfia, William Blair: “how you keep the momentum going forward… safeguards”; Chairman & CEO Niccol: “as we get more and more people close… we raise the standard… you also got to be in the coffeehouses… Because we don't have it all figured out.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated emphasis on sustainability and flow-through, including “macro backdrop,” “cost of goods,” and “labor dollars per operating week,” alongside acknowledgments like “really impressive” and “Congratulations again.” (Analyst questions)

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, pairing confidence with caution, including “we're also cognizant of the macro environment” and “we want to be cautious going forward.” (Executive VP, CFO Catherine Smith; Chairman & CEO Brian Niccol)

  • Compared with Q1, management language shifted from “we are clearly in the early stages of our turnaround” to “we believe this quarter reflects the turn in our turnaround,” while still reiterating “we know the path forward will not be linear.” (Chairman & CEO Niccol, Q1 vs. Q2)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language tightened upward: Q1 guided “3% or better global comp sales growth” and EPS of “$2.15 to $2.40,” while Q2 raised guidance to “5% growth or better” and EPS to “$2.25 and $2.45.” (Executive VP, CFO Catherine Smith)

  • China moved from “expect to close in the spring of this year” and held-for-sale accounting effects to a closed transaction and reporting change: “Beginning in Q3… Starbucks China will be deconsolidated” and “We will also cease our quarterly reporting on China stand-alone revenue and comps.” (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

  • Operational discussion evolved from Q1’s focus on early Green Apron scaling tools (e.g., “Green Dot Assist” and the “Grow program”) to Q2’s measurable execution claims, including “about 80% of our stores or better hitting the metrics” and an app roadmap with scheduled pickup. (Chairman & CEO Niccol)

Risks and concerns

  • “The current macro environment brings heightened uncertainty to our operating landscape and consumer behavior more broadly.” (Executive VP, CFO Catherine Smith)

  • Cost pressures highlighted in-quarter included “inflation largely related to tariffs and elevated coffee prices” and “greater-than-anticipated legal accruals,” with mitigation framed as “we expect these tariff and coffee pressures to moderate in the back half of fiscal 2026” and continued hedging lag effects. (Executive VP, CFO Smith)

Final takeaway

Management described Q2 as a turning point with top- and bottom-line growth, raised FY2026 comp and EPS guidance, and outlined near-term operating levers centered on Green Apron Service execution, app-based scheduled pickup, and faster throughput. The call also repositioned China as a post-close joint venture licensing structure starting Q3, with proceeds earmarked for debt reduction, while management reiterated prudence around macro uncertainty, tariffs, coffee costs, and in-quarter legal accruals.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Seagate signals minimum 20% annual revenue growth target, guides $3.45B revenue and $5 EPS for June quarter
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Seagate Technology (STX) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • “Seagate delivered a very strong March quarter,” and “we generated one of our highest ever levels of free cash flow at close to $1 billion,” said CEO William Mosley, framing the quarter as evidence of “the durability of demand and the leverage in our model.”
  • Mosley said “momentum continues to build for our Mozaic HAMR-based platforms,” adding that “2 of the world's largest CSPs now qualified on our 4+ terabyte per disk product,” and that qualification timelines were “in line with PMR products.”
  • Mosley stated the company is “entering a period of structural growth,” and said Seagate is “confiden[t] to significantly increase our annual revenue growth target from the low to mid-teens, to a minimum of 20% over the next few years.”
  • On demand visibility and allocation, Mosley said nearline “accounted for close to 90% of total Exabyte shipments,” and that Seagate has “nearline capacity almost fully allocated through calendar 2027,” while “finalizing build-to-order contracts…through the end of fiscal 2027.”
  • CFO Gianluca Romano said Seagate “posted very strong results for the March quarter,” reporting revenue of “$3.1 billion,” non-GAAP gross margin of “47%,” non-GAAP operating margin of “37.5%,” and non-GAAP EPS of “$4.10,” alongside “free cash flow generation…$953 million.”

Outlook

  • Romano guided June quarter revenue to “a range of $3.45 billion, plus or minus $100 million,” with non-GAAP operating expenses “approximately $295 million,” and non-GAAP operating margin “in the lower 40% range.”
  • Romano guided non-GAAP EPS to “$5 plus or minus $0.20,” based on “a tax rate of about 16%” and “non-GAAP diluted share count of 231 million shares,” including “estimated dilution from our 2028 convertible notes of approximately 3 million shares.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s outlook language, management shifted to broader confidence: Romano said, “we are confident in delivering quarterly revenue growth and margin expansion through fiscal 2027.”

Financial Results

  • Romano reported March-quarter mix skewed to data center, stating “the data center market accounted for 88% of exabyte shipments and 80% of revenue,” with “data center revenue…totaling $2.5 billion,” while “edge IoT…$612 million.”
  • Romano described profitability drivers as “continued execution of our long-term pricing strategy, along with improving product mix,” and said Seagate “expanded non-GAAP operating margin…to 37.5%,” with “non-GAAP operating expenses…$296 million or 9.5% of revenue.”
  • On cash and balance sheet, Romano said Seagate “retir[ed] $641 million in gross debt,” ended with “cash and cash equivalents…$1.1 billion,” and “gross debt…approximately $3.9 billion,” adding “Fitch recently upgraded Seagate credit to investment grade.”

Q&A

  • Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley: asked how “Agentic AI benefit[s] HDD demand”; CEO Mosley replied, “we are picking up confidence because of some of these new applications,” and tied demand to “enormous data sets” and “unstructured data, video data…hitting the storage tiers.”
  • Asiya Merchant, Citi: asked about cost reductions and HAMR targets; CEO Mosley said Mozaic transitions aim to “change as little as we can…with minimal change to the bill of materials,” and CFO Romano added cost reduction has been driven by “mix going to higher capacity drives” and “full utilization,” with future benefit from “very good increase in terabytes per unit…not adding more [bill of materials].”
  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: pressed on pricing acceleration; CEO Mosley said Seagate aims to “lock in with our customers and give them predictability,” and CFO Romano said, “we have now finalized our build-to-order for our fiscal '27…there are no changes to our pricing strategy.”
  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: asked on cash deployment and how much pricing is locked; CFO Romano said “the vast majority of our nearline capacity is allocated during the next 4 quarters,” and on capital allocation: “the majority will probably go to share buybacks,” while also addressing “about $400 million of the convertible…open.”
  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: asked whether Seagate is growing units; CEO Mosley answered, “no, we're still not growing units,” adding the “average number of heads per drive…may be increasing,” while CFO Romano noted “HAMR cycle time is a bit longer than PMR.”
  • Thomas O'Malley, Barclays: asked about prepayments in contracts; CFO Romano said, “we are not looking at prepayment in particular,” and CEO Mosley said Seagate is focused on “demand predictability.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated “pricing,” “margins,” and “units” pressure-testing; Morgan Stanley’s “how does Agentic AI benefit HDD demand?” and UBS’s unit-growth challenge (“are you, in fact, beginning to grow units”) reflected probing skepticism.
  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A; CEO Mosley said Seagate is “entering a period of structural growth,” and CFO Romano emphasized continuity: “there are no changes to our pricing strategy.”
  • Versus last quarter, management language moved from strong-demand visibility (“fully allocated through calendar year 2026”) to longer visibility and higher targets (“almost fully allocated through calendar 2027” and “a minimum of 20%” annual revenue growth target).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The biggest strategic change was Mosley’s explicit increase to “a minimum of 20%” annual revenue growth over the next few years, which was not stated in the prior quarter.
  • Contracting and allocation messaging extended: last quarter management said “nearline capacity is fully allocated through calendar year 2026,” while this quarter Mosley said nearline capacity is “almost fully allocated through calendar 2027,” with build-to-order contracts being finalized “through the end of fiscal 2027.”
  • Analysts stayed concentrated on pricing and margin sustainability in both quarters, but this quarter added more questions around “Agentic AI,” unit dynamics, and capital returns.

Risks and Concerns

  • Romano addressed geopolitical risk: “despite rising geopolitical tensions, including the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, we do not currently expect material impacts,” adding, “our teams acted quickly to mitigate supply and logistics disruption.”
  • On product allocation and trade-offs, CEO Mosley said, “we're not leaning too hard into the Mozaic 4 because some of the other product families are still doing quite well and needed for various customers.”

Final Takeaway

Seagate management described March-quarter results as “very strong,” raised its longer-term growth posture by increasing its annual revenue growth target to “a minimum of 20%,” and guided to June-quarter revenue of $3.45 billion ± $100 million and non-GAAP EPS of $5 ± $0.20. Leadership emphasized Mozaic HAMR qualification progress with large cloud customers, nearline allocation and build-to-order visibility through fiscal 2027, and capital allocation shifting from debt reduction toward “the majority…share buybacks,” while stating geopolitical disruptions are not expected to be material at this time.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
